section.title: Configuration with cURL
section.p: cURL cURL is a common command-line toolToolscURL for sending web requests to a server.

note.p: The example queries on these pages were mostly taken from the MarkLogic documentation pages, but have been adapted for Windows machines. Instead of shell scripts with various peculiarities, you can also put the code snippets into batch files and execute them.

section.p: MarkLogic listens on port 8002 with its Configuration Manager application. cURL requests for remote configuration can also be sent via this port.
To create a database, you issue the following cURL command:

messagepanel.typeofhazard: On my Windows machine, I could not execute the command as described in the MarkLogic docs[[fn:https://docs.marklogic.com/REST/POST/manage/v2/databases*Doku-Seite zum Anlegen einer MarkLogic Datenbank mit cURL]] because the JSON first had to be escaped with backslashes. Furthermore, in PowerShell the [[code:curl]] command is aliased to a Windows program[[fn:https://daniel.haxx.se/blog/2016/08/19/removing-the-powershell-curl-alias/*Anleitung zur Entfernung des curl Alias auf Windows]]
messagepanel.howtoavoid: Remedy: Escape the JSON string and remove the cURL alias on Windows.

section.p: Analogously, you create a "forest" that the database defined above requires:
The console acknowledges the successful event with these messages:
section.pre: HTTP/1.1 201 Created Location: /manage/v2/forests/12099403305847426321 Content-type: application/xml; charset=UTF-8 Cache-Control: no-cache Expires: -1 Server: MarkLogic Content-Length: 0 Connection: Keep-Alive Keep-Alive: timeout=5
section.p: The success message can also be easily verified in the Configuration Manager overview on port 8003. Now we can populate the new database with the MarkLogic Content Pump Tools for MarkLogicContent Pump .
To do this, we load the following example XML, located in a folder input-files, into the database:
section.pre: <test> <title>Test Datei</title> <chapter> <title>Test Kapitel 1</title> <content>Kapitel Inhalt 1</content> </chapter> <chapter> <title>Test Kapitel 2</title> <content>Kapitel Inhalt 2</content> </chapter> <chapter> <title>Test Kapitel 2</title> <content>Kapitel Inhalt 2</content> </chapter> </test>

note.p: We will correct the typo in the above XML in the following chapter.

section.p: This is done with the command:
section.pre: mlcp import -database xml-scrapper -host localhost -username admin -password admin -input_file_path input-files -input_file_type aggregates -aggregate_record_element chapter -output_collections /chapter -output_uri_prefix /chapter/ -output_uri_suffix .xml
section.p: Now we still need to create an application server in MarkLogic to be able to run our own XQuery scripts. In a file server-setup.json we define our server settings:
We send this to the server with the following cURL command:
In the web interface, we can verify that everything worked:

fig.title: Configuring an App Server on MarkLogic
fig.desc: In the [[code:Configure]] tab we can configure the App Server on MarkLogic.

section.p: The configuration can of course also be done via the web interface on port 8001, or via XQuery scripts through the console on port 8000. Some scripts for this purpose can be found on the MarkLogic Developer pages MarkLogic Admin API

After these preparations, we can now equip our app server with scripts, as described in the following section. We place the scripts in the root directory c:\xquery, which we defined above, and can call them via a web request, e.g. like this:

section.title: Implementation as XQuery Script
section.p: In this section, we will generate an HTML page with a table of contents from the previously loaded data.
Let's start with a script book.xqy in the directory C:\xml-scrapper

fig.title: First output of our small XQuery script for a website
fig.desc: Result: The chapters of the web page are written out one after another. This is of course not yet optimal

messagepanel.typeofhazard: Here we notice that we included Chapter 2 twice. This is a typo.
messagepanel.howtoavoid: We will take care of this error later.

section.p: Now we want to split the individual pages across different web pages and display a table of contents on a cover page.
Compared to an XSLT solution, you will notice that trying to use the XPath function fn:position() is futile. Instead, you use the at keyword in the for loop.
On the initial cover page, a linked table of contents is now displayed:

fig.title: Second output of our small XQuery script for a website
fig.desc: The second step of our web application is a table of contents with linked chapters

section.p: The request parameter $page declared in the script is now evaluated to generate the chapter pages.

Here the at keyword is interesting, as it allows you to capture the position in the loop. fn:position(), as commonly used in XSLT, would not work here. That we can evaluate conditional statements in functional languages as expressions, we have already learned here, cf. the sx:decimal cast instruction for type conversion.
Our website would actually be perfect if it weren't for the faulty data import, having imported Chapter 2 twice. To clean up the data, a data migration is necessary.

section.title: Web Apps with Multiple Databases
section.p: In MarkLogic, each app server is assigned to exactly one content database. All data that the web application accesses should be present in it.
However, it is possible via a small "hack" to query other databases within the same MarkLogic web app. For this, you use the xdmp:eval-in function.
As the name suggests, an expression is evaluated in another database.
The example on the MarkLogic documentation page xdmp:eval-in looks as follows:
section.pre:
xquery version "0.9-ml" declare namespace my='http://mycompany.com/test'
let $s := "xquery version '0.9-ml' declare namespace my='http://mycompany.com/test' define variable $my:x as xs:string external concat('hello ', $my:x)" return (: evaluate the query string $s using the variables supplied as the second parameter to xdmp:eval :) xdmp:eval-in($s, xdmp:database("Documents"), (xs:QName("my:x"), "world"))
=> hello world
section.p: A practical example from the real world would look like this:
Here a previously set SQL View, cf. chapter SQL Views in MarkLogic, which by default resides in the schema database, is deleted from the system.

section.title: Data Correction with the Console
section.p: To correct the faulty data from the previous chapter, we open a console session on port 8000:

note.p: We notice in the following figure that the mlcp command has prefixed the document-name with the absolute path of the file in the file system of the importing machine.

fig.title: MarkLogic Console Session with a Collection Iteration
fig.desc: In the console, we can list the documents stored in the collection.

section.p: We see that we created Chapter 2 twice in the collection. So we need to correct the 3rd element in the collection:

messagepanel.typeofhazard: The semicolon at the end of the statement is a MarkLogic-specific feature and indicates that this statement should be executed within a transaction.
messagepanel.howtoavoid: In other XQuery implementations, this feature may not be available.

section.p: After this correction, the data should be correct again and our web app is finished...
